title: How huntingtin proteins travel in the brain
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 25 March 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear about the therapeutic potential of adhesion G-protein-coupled receptors, discuss the key to developing RNA-based drugs and learn why endometriosis must be seen as a whole-body disorder.
Proteinous tubes connect Rhes protein-expressing neurons to shuttle huntingtin protein in Huntington’s disease. (Emaad Mirza, Florida Atlantic University)

How huntingtin proteins travel in the brain

Mutant huntingtin protein, which causes the neurodegenerative disorder Huntington’s disease, travels through the brain using tiny ‘tunneling nanotubes’, research has revealed. Researchers found that a protein called Rhes binds with a protein for cellular acidity regulation, SLC4A7, to build tube-like structures creating a highway to shuttle huntingtin from neuron to neuron. Interrupting this pathway minimised the spread of huntingtin in the brain and offers a potential druggable target.

Genetic Engineering & Biotechnology News | 8 min read
 Reference: Science Advances paper

FDA approvals on the horizon

Next quarter, the US Food and Drug administration is set to approve a suite of notable drugs. This includes baxdrostat for uncontrolled and treatment-resistant hypertension, which would be the first new oral drug that can be used in conjunction with other medication in this space in decades. Orforglipron is an oral, once-daily GLP-1 for obesity, setting it apart from the predominantly injectable GLP-1 drugs currently on the market. Vepdegestrant for ESR1-mutant metastatic breast cancer is up for consideration, but might be limited for use in people going for a second round of treatment with an ESR1 mutation.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 6 min read

Review of the month
(Sun, JP., Xiao, P. & Liebscher, I./Nat. Rev. Drug. Discov.)

The therapeutic potential of adhesion GPCRs

Adhesion G-protein-coupled receptors (aGPCRs) have immense potential as therapeutics, argue pharmacologist Jin-Peng Sun, medical researcher Peng Xiao and biochemist Ines Liebscher. aGPCRs are membrane proteins with two signalling pathways (cis pictured left, trans right) implicated in many diseases such as cancer, psychiatric disorders and autoimmune disease. No aGPCR-targetting drugs are currently approved, but a number of animal studies have shown promise as well as one human study. To realise the target's potential will involve continued exploration of its diverse signalling pathways, pharmacodynamics and other drug development considerations, write the authors. (45 min read | Nature Reviews Drug Discovery)

Features and opinion

The key to discovery of RNA-based drugs

A research program in China aims to link the study of RNA biology to drug discovery, to fast-track a shift towards more RNA-based drugs. Despite the success of mRNA vaccines and RNA interference-based drugs, drug development in the area has faced consistent roadblocks: the properties of RNA can contribute to drug toxicity and finding targets poses a challenge. RNA-based drugs also struggle with delivery, particularly outside of the liver. The National Natural Science Foundation of China aims to support cross-disciplinary research aimed at producing RNA-centered drugs. To overcome bottlenecks, researchers need to focus on RNA research aimed at drug production, collaborate across chemistry, materials science and bioengineering and use AI, argue the authors of this comment in Nature Chemical Biology.

Nature Chemical Biology | 12 min read

Endometriosis: a whole-body disorder

Endometriosis must be viewed as a whole-body, immune-driven disorder in order to find effective treatments, argue biochemist and immunologist April Rees and data scientist Laura Elizabeth Cowley. Endometriosis occurs when the lining of the uterus grows elsewhere within the body. It has historically been viewed as a gynaecological condition, but this underplays its complexity and means that most treatments target the reproductive system, write the authors. Treatments looking at widespread immune dysfunction could offer more effective long-term relief.

The Conversation | 6 min read

Infographic of the week
Researchers have found that intravenous administration of a nonpathogenic virus, oncolytic alphavirus M1 (OVM), suppresses the growth of glioblastoma, a lethal brain cancer, and also reverses the cancer’s suppression of the immune system. OVM triggers an immune response to cell death (ICD), restoration of the immune system, tumour microenvironment (TME) reprogramming and proliferation of immune cells that specifically target the tumour. (Cellular & Molecular Immunology | 14 min read) (Dieli, C., Di Simone, M., Dieli, F. et al./Cell. Mol. Immunol.)

Quote of the week

"It doesn't mean that there aren't surprises or miracles. They do happen, but you have to kiss many frogs before you meet the prince."

Chemist Kelly Chibale says that drug discovery takes time and patience. He currently runs the Holistic Drug Discovery and Development Centre at the University of Cape Town in South Africa. (NPR | 11 min read)

Another book for the ‘to read’ list! Animate by science writer Michael Bond looks to take on human exceptionalism, the concept of which is constantly being eroded by scientific discovery.

Please send other book recommendations — they don’t have to be science-based — and your feedback to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham

summary.overview: 이번 호 뉴스레터는 뇌에서의 헌팅틴 단백질 이동 메커니즘, FDA 승인 약물, aGPCR의 치료 잠재력, 그리고 자궁내막증의 전신 질환으로서의 특징을 다룬 주요 기사 4편을 소개합니다.
covered_item_titles: How huntingtin proteins travel in the brain
The therapeutic potential of adhesion GPCRs

article_briefings.title: How huntingtin proteins travel in the brain
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 산만한 헌팅틴 단백질이 뇌에서 '튜닝 나노관'을 통해 이동하며, Rhes 단백질과의 결합이 이 과정에 관여하는 것으로 밝혀졌습니다.

article_briefings.title: FDA approvals on the horizon
article_briefings.briefing_ko: FDA가 다음 분기 신약 승인을 발표할 예정으로, 고혈압 치료제인 바크로스타트가 포함된 주목할 만한 약물들이 대상입니다.

article_briefings.title: The therapeutic potential of adhesion GPCRs
article_briefings.briefing_ko: aGPCR은 암, 정신병, 자가면역 질환 치료에 잠재력이 크지만, 현재 승인된 약물은 없으며 동물 및 인간 연구에서 기대감을 보이고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Endometriosis: a whole-body disorder
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 자궁내막증은 전신 면역 관련 질환으로 간주되어, 새로운 치료법 개발을 위한 전반적인 접근이 필요하다고 제안되었습니다.
